content.full_text:
April 5, 1995


Paul Sickert, Esq.
California Lottery Commission
Legal Department
600 N. 10th Street
Sacramento, California  95814

                                                Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                                     Our File No. A-95-054

Dear Mr. Sickert:

     You have asked for advice regarding the required disclosure
of economic interests by members of the California State Lottery
Commission under the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

                              QUESTION

     Under the provisions of Section 87200 and Commission
Regulation 18720, are members of the California State Lottery
Commission considered "other public officials who manage public
investments" who must disclose their economic interests on Form
721 instead of Form 730?

                              CONCLUSION

     Based on the facts presented, the members of the California
State Lottery Commission are considered "other public officials
who manage public investments."  These individuals are subject to
the disclosure requirements of Article 2, Chapter 7 of the Act and
should file a Statement of Economic Interests Form 721.


_________________________

     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-054
Page 2


FACTS²

The California State Lottery Commission (the "CSLC") is
responsible for effectuating the purposes of the California State
Lottery Act of 1984, (the "CSLA," Government Code Sections 8880 et
seq.). The CSLC is empowered to promulgate rules and regulations
concerning the California State Lottery (the "Lottery").

The distribution of revenues generated by the sale of lottery
tickets is regulated by the CSLA, which statutorily set the
permitted allocation of these revenues from ticket sales. The
CSLA requires that 50 percent of revenues be returned to the
public as prizes, at least 34 percent of revenues be allocated to
education, no more than 16 percent of revenues be used for
administrative expenses of the Lottery, and if less than 16
percent is used for administration, the difference go to
education.

The Lottery annuitizes lottery prizes of more than $1
million, paying them over a period of time, typically twenty
years. Early in its existence, the CSLC made a policy decision to
fund the annuitized prizes in a particular manner, and this policy
decision is reflected in the CSLC's regulations. The policy
provides that to fund the prize payment stream, prize funds on
hand are used to purchase a "ladder" of zero coupon bonds which
fully fund the annual prize payments. These bonds are purchased
for the Lottery by the State Treasurer's Office.

Since the policy decision was made to fund annuitized prizes
in this manner, the policy has operated without the commissioners'
involvement. However, the CSLC is the body responsible for any
amendments to the regulations concerning the "choice of
investment" to pay the annuitized prizes.

ANALYSIS

Section 87200 of the Act lists those officials, including
"other public officials who manage public investments," who are
subject to the disclosure requirements of Article 2, Chapter 7 of
the Act and must file extensive disclosure of their economic
interests on Form 721.

Regulation 18720(b) defines "other public officials who
manage public investments" to include:

     (1) Members of boards and commissions, including
pension and retirement boards or commissions, or of
committees thereof, who exercise responsibility for the
management of public investments....

_____________________
² These facts are based on your letters dated February 7, 1995,
and February 27, 1995, and telephone conversations with you on
January 31, 1995, and February 27, 1995.

Our File No. A-95-054
Page 3


Under Regulation 18720(b), the members of the CSLC are
members of a commission who exercise responsibility for the
management of public investments as discussed below.

Regulation 18720(a) sets forth the following definitions to
assist in interpreting the phrase "management of public
investments":

(1) "Public investments" means the investment of
public moneys in real estate, securities, or other
economic interests for the production of revenue or
other financial return.

(2) "Public moneys" means all moneys belonging to,
received by, or held by, the state, or any city, county,
town, district, or public agency therein, or by an
officer thereof acting in his official capacity, and
includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidence of
indebtedness, trust funds held by public pension and
retirement systems, deferred compensation funds held for
investments by public agencies, and public moneys held
by a financial institution under a trust indenture to
which a public agency is a party.

(3) "Management of public investments" means the
following non-ministerial functions: directing the
investment of public moneys; formulating or approving
investment policies; approving or establishing
guidelines for asset allocations; or approving
investment transactions."

The revenues generated from the sale of lottery tickets
constitute public moneys under the above definition. Under the
language of the regulation, the CSLC's decision to fund the
revenues in a particular manner constitutes directing the
investment of public moneys. This is true even though the CSLC
does not directly purchase the bonds for the annuitized payments.

In addition, the CSLC has formulated a policy for the choice
of investment, which is reflected in a CSLC regulation. Although
that policy has not changed, the CSLC is empowered to amend or
repeal the regulation which instituted that policy.3 Thus, the
CSLC formulates or approves investment policies within the meaning
of the regulation as well.


3 We are unpersuaded by the argument that the current commission
members are not responsible for major policy decisions of prior
commissions which continue with the current commission's consent
and under the current commission's term of office.

Our File No. A-95-054
Page 4


     Therefore, the CSLC manages public investments within the
regulation's definition and the members of the CSLC should file a
Statement of Economic Interests Form 721.

     If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Luisa Menchaca
                                        Counsel, Legal Division
